name: On top of steep hill
short_description: BLM rockhounding site near Prescott offering agate and jasper collecting on a gentle hillside. Free public access with 25 lb daily limit for personal use.
description:
This BLM location near Prescott provides excellent opportunity for rockhounders seeking Arizona's classic desert gemstones. The gentle sloping terrain at 3,140 feet elevation makes access relatively straightforward for collectors of varying experience levels. Agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens are the primary finds here, weathering out from the surrounding volcanic geology that dominates this region of Yavapai County.

The area sits within Arizona's Transitional Zone, where Tertiary volcanic activity created the silica-rich deposits that formed the microcrystalline quartz varieties found today. The exposed hillside geology reveals layers of volcanic rhyolite and dacite that have weathered over millions of years, concentrating colorful agate and jasper nodules at the surface and in shallow washes. This natural weathering process continuously exposes new specimens.

Expect to find specimens ranging from thumbnail size to hand-sized pieces, with colors varying from translucent white and tan to deep orange, red, and banded patterns. The gentle slopes allow for comfortable walking and scanning of the surface for exposed material. Nearby washes including Antelope Creek offer additional exploration opportunities within a reasonable hiking radius.

This location is ideal for both beginners and experienced rockhounders. The public BLM access means no fees or permits are required, though collectors must follow standard recreational collection guidelines. Spring and fall weather makes this an excellent season to work the site without intense desert heat.
history: This region of Yavapai County was shaped by extensive Tertiary volcanism between 25 and 35 million years ago, when silica-rich hydrothermal fluids circulated through the volcanic rocks and deposited the microcrystalline quartz that forms agate and chalcedony nodules. The overlying volcanic deposits have since weathered and eroded, exposing these gemstones at the surface where rockhounders can readily collect them today.
tips: Scan the gentle hillside slopes for the characteristic weathered surface of agate nodules, which often appear as rounded stones with a waxy luster. Look especially along the shallow washes and eroded areas where water has concentrated heavier material. Bring a small hand lens to identify chalcedony banding and check for translucency, which indicates quality specimens. Focus your search on the exposed volcanic rock faces and adjacent soil areas where specimens weather out naturally - avoid digging or breaking rock, as surface collecting yields plenty of material.
terrain_notes: Elevation 3,140 feet with gentle sloping terrain and scattered volcanic rock outcrops. Vegetation includes typical Arizona foothill scrub and desert shrubs. Walking from the parking area to productive collecting ground is approximately 0.25 to 0.5 miles across exposed hillside. Footing is generally stable on compacted soil and loose volcanic rock, though care should be taken on steeper slope sections. No maintained trails, but the open terrain allows for flexible route selection.
best_season: October - April. Desert heat at lower Arizona elevations makes summer months (May-September) challenging, with daytime temperatures often exceeding 95 degrees F. Fall through spring offers comfortable temperatures for sustained fieldwork and exploration.
written_directions: From Prescott, take State Route 69 south toward Dewey for approximately 18 miles. Continue on SR 69 or local roads following GPS coordinates 34.153681, -112.763663, which places you on public BLM land on a hillside approximately 32 miles from downtown Prescott. High-clearance vehicles are recommended due to rough access roads in this area. Consult detailed topographic maps or GPS devices, as directional roads in this region are not heavily signed. The nearest reliable services are in Prescott.
road_conditions: Rough unpaved access road requiring high-clearance vehicle. Some washboard surface present. Standard passenger vehicles may have difficulty during wet conditions.
parking_notes: Informal parking area or pullout typical of BLM public land. Capacity limited. Arrive early during peak season.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection allowed. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Surface collection only - do not dig or break rocks. Respect the natural landscape and leave no trace of your visit.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Surface collection only
No rock breaking or digging
Leave no trace
nearest_services: Prescott, approximately 32 miles north. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers and collectors. Rough unpaved access road to parking area requires high-clearance vehicle. Once at the site, walking is straightforward on open hillside without technical climbing. Sturdy footwear recommended due to sharp volcanic rock. Not suitable for wheelchairs or those with limited mobility due to uneven terrain and rough vehicle access.

faq.question: What types of agates and jaspers can I find rockhounding near Prescott, Arizona?
faq.answer: This BLM site near Prescott is known for producing colorful banded agate, red and orange jasper, and translucent chalcedony specimens. The volcanic geology of Yavapai County yields specimens with distinctive banding patterns and varying opacities, from milky white to deep rust red. Quartz varieties including smoky quartz are also present. Most specimens found are thumbnail to hand-sized, perfect for collectors of all levels.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free on BLM land in Arizona, and what are the collection limits?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding on this BLM land is completely free with no permit required. The only restriction is the 25 lb per day personal use limit set by FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act). This is plenty for a productive day of collecting agate and jasper. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Prescott at this location?
faq.answer: October through April offers ideal conditions. Winter temperatures are mild at 3,140 feet elevation, and spring brings comfortable collecting weather. Avoid May through September when desert heat can exceed 95 degrees F, making extended fieldwork uncomfortable and potentially dangerous. April and October are particularly excellent with perfect temperatures and lower crowd levels.

faq.question: Can beginners find good specimens rockhounding on top of steep hill near Prescott?
faq.answer: Absolutely. This location is beginner-friendly with easy terrain and abundant surface specimens that require no specialized tools or experience to collect. Simply walk the gentle hillside slopes, scan for the characteristic waxy luster of agate nodules, and pick up quality material. The 25 lb daily limit ensures even beginners can fill their collections with impressive finds on a single visit.

faq.question: What tools and safety equipment do I need for rockhounding in this Prescott-area BLM zone?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots or shoes for rough volcanic rock terrain, a collection bag or bucket, and a small hand lens to examine specimens for quality and banding. Hat and sunscreen are essential even in cooler months. Carry plenty of water (2-3 liters minimum) due to the remote location. A map or GPS device is recommended since cell service is spotty in this area.

faq.question: Are dogs allowed at this BLM rockhounding site near Prescott, and is it family-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome on BLM land, making this location great for family trips with pets. The easy terrain and gentle slopes are suitable for children ages 6 and up, and the short walking distance to productive collecting areas keeps families engaged. The combination of free access, abundant specimens, and pet-friendly status makes this an excellent choice for family outings.

meta_title: Prescott Agate Rockhounding - Free BLM Site
meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony on free BLM land near Prescott, Arizona. No permit needed. 25 lb daily limit. Easy terrain, beginner-friendly rockhounding.
